papers.title: A deep-learning-informed prior and Bayesian model for differential AP-MS interactome analysis
papers.abstract: Affinity-purification mass spectrometry (AP-MS) maps a bait proteins partners, but every purification also captures abundant non-specific background that masks genuine interactors. Established tools such as SAINTexpress and CompPASS score one evidence type, treat correlated signals as independent, and ignore prior knowledge of likely interactions. BayesInteractomics, an open-source Julia framework, addresses both limitations by combining machine learning with Bayesian statistics. A neural network trained on protein structures predicts direct binding. A calibrated meta-learner turns this into a
papers.summary: This paper introduces BayesInteractomics, a new computational framework that combines deep learning with Bayesian statistics to improve the analysis of affinity-purification mass spectrometry (AP-MS) data. By using a neural network trained on protein structures to predict direct binding and incorporating this as prior knowledge, the method outperforms existing tools in identifying genuine protein-protein interactions while reducing false positives from non-specific background.

papers.title: scJET: Full-gene Space Single-cell Expression Generation with Patch-based Transformer Modeling
papers.abstract: Most single-cell generative models rely on highly variable genes (HVGs) or low-dimensional latent representations, limiting their capacity to capture the complexity of full-gene features. We present scJET, a patch-based Transformer denoising framework that operates in full-gene space. scJET preserves global manifold structure, local neighborhood statistics, and gene-level expression programs. By combining scalable patch tokenization with full-gene denoising, scJET provides an efficient framework for transcriptome-wide single-cell matrix generation.
papers.summary: scJET is a new deep learning model that can generate realistic single-cell gene expression data across the entire transcriptome, not just a subset of highly variable genes. It uses a patch-based Transformer approach to efficiently handle the full gene space while preserving both global and local biological patterns. This provides a more complete and scalable framework for generating synthetic single-cell data for downstream analyses.

papers.title: SPECTER-Based Semantic Triage of Biomedical Literature for Systematic Reviews in Mutational Signature Analysis
papers.abstract: Systematic reviews in computational biology require screening large heterogeneous bibliographic sets, especially when topics span computational methods, cancer genomics and statistical modelling. This paper presents a reproducible semantic triage pipeline that combines SPECTER scientific-document embeddings, research-question similarity, proposal-summary similarity and domain keyword coverage to rank candidate studies for systematic review screening. The pipeline was evaluated on 2,231 Covidence records, including 120 final included studies (prevalence = 5.38%), against keyword-only, TF-IDF, B
papers.summary: This paper presents a reproducible pipeline that uses SPECTER scientific-document embeddings and similarity scoring to automatically rank and prioritize biomedical literature for systematic reviews, specifically in mutational signature analysis. The method outperforms keyword-only and TF-IDF approaches in screening efficiency, reducing the manual review burden by identifying relevant studies more effectively.

papers.title: BBBP_Atlas: Unified Interpretable Modeling of Blood Brain Barrier Permeability across Small Molecules and Peptides
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of blood-brain barrier permeability (BBBP) is essential for central nervous system drug discovery, yet existing models are often limited by their reliance on predefined physicochemical descriptors, small-molecule-centered training sets, or conformation-dependent representations, which restricts their transferability across chemically diverse modalities especially peptides. In addition, publicly available BBBP datasets remain fragmented, inconsistently standardized, and weakly controlled for molecular redundancy, increasing the risk of data leakage and overestimated model pe
papers.summary: This paper introduces BBBP_Atlas, a unified and interpretable model that predicts blood-brain barrier permeability for both small molecules and peptides, overcoming limitations of existing models that rely on predefined descriptors or small-molecule-only training data. The authors also address data fragmentation and leakage issues by standardizing public BBBP datasets, improving model reliability for CNS drug discovery.

papers.title: Mutational bias shapes protein evolution across RNA viruses
papers.abstract: Mutational biases can influence genome composition, but their contribution to protein evolution remains difficult to quantify. Here we utilize a nearly neutral framework that translates nucleotide mutational spectra into expected amino acid substitution patterns and equilibrium amino acid compositions. Using SARS-CoV-2 as a model system, we show that the viral mutational spectrum explains more than 50% of the variation in observed single-nucleotide amino acid substitutions and predicts the overall direction of proteome-wide amino acid composition change during the COVID-19 pandemic. The predic
papers.summary: This study demonstrates that mutational biases, not just natural selection, play a major role in shaping protein evolution in RNA viruses. Using SARS-CoV-2 as a model, the authors show that the virus's mutational spectrum explains over 50% of observed amino acid substitutions and accurately predicts proteome-wide composition changes during the pandemic. This work provides a quantitative framework for disentangling mutation and selection in viral evolution.
